system-admin-guide.title: welcome
system-admin-guide.content: Welcome to GDI system administration This guide is for system administrators responsible for deploying, configuring, and maintaining the GDI User Portal platform and its associated services. The GDI platform is part of the Genomic Data Infrastructure (GDI) project and the 1+ Million Genomes Initiative. It provides the technical infrastructure that enables federated and secure cross-border access to genomic datasets across European countries. As a system administrator, you can deploy and maintain the platform components—including the frontend, backend microservices, CKAN data catalogue, Keycloak authentication, and supporting services—that power Europe's largest network of genomic data for healthcare research and clinical purposes. What would you like to do? Deploy infrastructure Set up the platform components and hosting environment. Install the platform Install frontend, backend services, CKAN extensions, and supporting components. Set up authentication Configure user access with Keycloak and LS-AAI integration. Manage roles and permissions Control user access levels within the data catalogue system. Manage data and services Administer CKAN, configure harvesters, and set up FAIR Data Points. Monitor and maintain the system Track performance, audit security, and manage platform updates.

developer-guide.title: platform overview
developer-guide.content: Platform overview The GDI User Portal is a distributed system composed of multiple independent services working together to provide dataset discovery and access management capabilities. :::tip Architecture at a glance For detailed architecture diagrams and component interactions, see: GDI application architecture. ::: Core components User-facing layer - Frontend (Next.js): React-based web application providing the user interface - TypeScript, Next.js 14, TailwindCSS - Server-side rendering (SSR) for performance - Responsive design for mobile and desktop API layer - Kong Gateway: API gateway handling routing, authentication, and rate limiting - Routes requests between frontend and backend services - Enforces security policies - Provides observability through logging Backend services (Java/Quarkus) - Dataset Discovery Service (DDS): Mediates frontend requests to CKAN - REST API for dataset search and retrieval - Transforms CKAN responses to frontend-friendly format - Implements business logic for dataset filtering - Access Management Service (AMS): Manages access request workflows - Integrates with REMS for application management - Tracks application status and approvals - Provides notification capabilities Data catalogue - CKAN: Open-source data catalogue system - PostgreSQL database for metadata storage - Solr for full-text search - Custom GDI extensions make it possible to support DCAT-AP 3 and HealthDCAT - Harvester extensions for collecting external datasets Supporting services - Keycloak: Identity and access management (IAM) - OpenID Connect (OIDC) authentication - Integration with LS-AAI federated login - PKCE flow support for secure authentication - REMS: Resource Entitlement Management System - Access request workflow engine - Application approval processes - Integration via REST API - FAIR Data Point (FDP): Metadata repository - Implements FAIR principles - Provides harvestable metadata endpoints - DCAT-AP compatible outputs Technology stack | Component | Technology | Language | | ----------- | -------------- | ----------- | | Frontend | Next.js 14 | TypeScript | | DDS/AMS | Quarkus | Java 17+ | | CKAN | CKAN 2.11.3 | Python 3.9+ | | API Gateway | Kong | Lua/Docker | | Database | PostgreSQL 14+ | SQL | | Search | Apache Solr 8+ | - | | Cache | Redis 7+ | - | | IAM | Keycloak 22+ | - | Next steps Now that you understand the platform, learn about repository structure to find where code lives.

developer-guide.title: 3 ckan architecture
developer-guide.content: CKAN architecture The GDI platform extends CKAN, an open-source data catalogue platform, with custom plugins for DCAT-AP 3 support, OIDC authentication, and harvesting. CKAN extension structure Key components of the extension include: Key concepts - IPlugin interface: CKAN extensions implement the interface to hook into CKAN's core functionality. - Scheming extension: GDI uses to define custom metadata schemas in JSON/YAML format. - Templates: Jinja2 templates override CKAN's default UI. - Validators: Custom validators ensure data quality for metadata fields. Database structure Understand CKAN's PostgreSQL database schema to develop extensions that interact with dataset metadata and harvesters. Dataset tables In demo schema table , columns are core fields of CKAN, and for every other field. Every field which extends the core schema lands in the extra table. The Scheming extension of Civity allows more flexibility for managing extra fields than CKAN core default functionality, but still such a field is converted to a string and lands in the extra table. It is possible to write a mapper to and map all the extra fields. For DCAT there is an official extension: https://github.com/ckan/ckanext-dcat#json-dcat-harvester It is compatible with DCAT-AP v1.1 and 2.1 !Database mapping Harvester tables In the database, the following tables are dedicated to store harvester-related information: - - harvested sources are defined - - the table where all the objects from a source are saved. Data from a source are stored in and from there will be converted to a CKAN dataset. Harvesters are also saved to the table of harvest. After changing something in the database directly, trigger re-indexing in Solr via search-index rebuild of CLI.

Clone the GDI DCAT extension repository: 2. Add the new field to the relevant DCAT schema. - For DCAT-AP fields, update files such as . - For HealthDCAT fields, update files such as or . - For dataset series fields, review and . - Use appropriate field types (e.g., text, repeating subfield, URI). - Follow examples from other fields for consistency. 3. Extend the existing mapping depending on the DCAT-AP version. Modify the mapping files located in the directory: . :::tip Example Review existing multi-valued fields such as in the GDI DCAT fork for mapping and test patterns. ::: 4. Fix the corresponding unit tests. 5. Create a pull request to . - Include unit tests for the new fields. - Ensure compatibility across different DCAT-AP versions. 6. Update the following repositories after a new release. Update development and production Dockerfiles in these repositories (order is important): - https://github.com/GenomicDataInfrastructure/gdi-userportal-ckanext-fairdatapoint - https://github.com/GenomicDataInfrastructure/gdi-userportal-ckan-docker Check if CKAN locally works with the new added fields by harvesting an example FDP. :::info Note Always take into account the mapping from CKAN → DCAT in addition to DCAT → CKAN. ::: CKAN scheming schemas Use the GDI User Portal CKAN extension when adding fields that should appear in CKAN dataset forms or be stored as CKAN package fields: 1. Clone the GDI User Portal CKAN extension repository: 2. Update the active scheming schemas. The Docker setup configures these files through : - - 3. Update GDI-specific presets if the field needs custom rendering or validation. - 4. Check the Docker scheming configuration. The active configuration is maintained in , in . For field keys and schema syntax, see the CKAN scheming documentation and Work with CKAN schemas. Solr search integration To make new CKAN fields searchable via Solr, modify the file. To add and configure a searchable field: 1. Define the field type and name. In the top part of the file, define the type and name of the new field. The type specifies how Solr will handle the data in the field (e.g., as , , , etc.). Find the section in where other fields are defined, and then add your new field with its corresponding type. Example: In this example, is the field name with type. The attribute enables searching, while allows retrieval in results. :::note Indexing vs Storing - indexed="true": The field can be used in searches. - stored="true": The field can be retrieved in search results. ::: 2. Add the field to search. Alternative text: In the lower part of the file, add a directive to include the new field in the search index. This allows Solr to use the contents of the new field when performing searches. Example: This example maps the to the text field, which Solr uses for full-text searches. By adding the directive, you're instructing Solr to include the contents of in the search index. 3. Release a new version and update. developer-guide.title: 4 work with ckan schemas
developer-guide.content: Work with CKAN schemas Configure, replace, and maintain dataset schemas in CKAN with JSON or YAML format. In this guide > Schema format and fields > Change schema in a running CKAN instance > Declare multiple schemas Schema format and fields Define CKAN schemas as JSON or YAML files. Civity continues to use .json schemas, while the official documentation includes many YAML references. A field in a CKAN schema .json file uses the following format: where: - is CKAN field id - is UI field representation - is text that appears beneath a field in the UI next to the icon. Square brackets are omitted and in this particular example contain information about how this field maps to DCAT-AP. Read more about these mappings and custom fields in CKAN DB Structure and Fields Mapping Strategy. :::tip Schema documentation Refer to the documentation for field keys and schema specifications: CKAN GitHub official repository.. ::: Additional field keys include: - : Defines repeating field groups within a dataset schema. May be useful for handling cardinality issues. Not currently used by Civity. - : Controls which multi-page form stage displays the field (recently added feature). Determines field placement across the dataset creation workflow stages. Not currently used by Civity. - : Defines static dropdown options as a list of dictionaries with and keys. - : Generates dropdown options dynamically from an API or another schema field. For example, you can reference another schema and retrieve field values from it. - : Applies predefined field configurations such as , , or . Enables automatic validation and rendering without defining custom snippets. - : Defines custom field input rendering using Jinja2 templates. Interacts with CKAN UI to customize field representation for data input. Use when presets don't provide sufficient control. - : Defines custom field display rendering using Jinja2 templates. Interacts with CKAN UI to control how stored values appear (e.g., rendering email addresses as clickable links). - : Overrides the RDF property representation for the field. Useful when mapping CKAN fields to specific DCAT vocabulary terms. For example, mapping to DCAT: - : Validates field data using CKAN's built-in validation functions (see the complete list of available functions). Custom validators can also be added. By default, minimal validation is applied: (accepts missing values) and (text encoding). - When defining custom validators, explicitly include these default validators if needed. - To create custom validation functions, implement an extension with the interface that returns your validation function from the method. See the validation functions documentation for implementation details. - : Handles deserialization when retrieving fields from the database. Complex data structures assigned to a field are converted to strings when stored in the database. This property defines validators for extracting the field from the database and converting it back to its object representation. Change schema in a running CKAN instance Define the schema in by setting where forms part of the CKAN Command Line Interface. Review the documentation for more information on usage. In a running Docker container, the configuration file defines this in the parameter. This should reference an extension under the directory of the repository. To replace the schema, run: Changing the file triggers a CKAN update, applying changes almost immediately. Define schemas in the following format: :. Clone the extension under . The path from the example above () resolves to the following: !Scheming Declare multiple schemas Scheming uses the IDatasetForm interface to override schemas. In CKAN.ini file Define multiple (space-separated) values in . CKAN core and Civity-extended CKAN differ in behaviour: core CKAN picks the latest schema when two share the same field. Civity improves this by merging schemas of the same type, reflecting the order specified in in the CKAN UI field order. The parameter defines rules for merging. When set to , and schemas to merge share fields, the system prioritises definitions from the latest schema. Fields are never deleted; they can only be added or modified. To delete a field, undeclare it or explicitly set it to empty. The system implements this behaviour in () and documents it only at code level. developer-guide.title: 1 harvester overview
developer-guide.content: Harvester overview The Fair Data Point harvester in CKAN processes datasets through three stages: - - - Gather stage During , the harvester requests all available resources from a source and generates a unique guid for each resource. - GUID generation The harvester generates the following GUIDs: - for a catalog - for a dataset where is an FDP reference URL (subject URL) of the resource. For example, for this dataset: https://health-ri.sandbox.semlab-leiden.nl/dataset/d7129d28-b72a-437f-8db0-4f0258dd3c25, the CKAN harvester GUID will be: - Status assignment The harvester queries CKAN database for guids harvested from the same source before: where is the harvester source id of the current job. Based on these two lists of guids, a harvest object is created and assigned with status , or : - where are ids from CKAN table for a given source (the result of the query above) - = resources that appear in the harvest but not in the database - = resources that exist in both with potential updates For resources marked as , the harvester sets the status of harvest objects to , so they stay in the database but are not shown. Fetch stage During , data for resources with or status are collected and parsed from the source. Import stage During , resources are deleted, updated, or inserted to the CKAN database based on the harvest object status: - : The harvester deletes datasets by calling - : Datasets are inserted into CKAN - : Existing datasets are updated with new metadata Based on the CKAN documentation, it is possible to configure a CKAN instance to prevent updating of certain fields. :::info Note - CKAN treats datasets as "new" if you delete and reconfigure their harvester source. - If deletion fails during the , the dataset remains hidden in the database permanently. - When you move a dataset between catalogues in FDP (by updating ), CKAN treats it as a new dataset because the GUID includes the catalogue id. :::

developer-guide.title: 2 test locally
developer-guide.content: Test harvester locally Test DCAT harvesting in CKAN by setting up a local nginx web server. This guide assumes you have CKAN running with the previously configured docker-compose. Use hosted test data You can also test harvesting with the Health-RI harvesting test data repository. 1. Find the CKAN sysadmin credentials in the file: 2. Log in to CKAN with those credentials. 3. Create an organisation. CKAN requires an organisation before you can create a harvest source. The name can be any local test name, for example . 4. Create a harvest source for the test data from : | Field | Value | | -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ | | URL | | | Title | Any title you can recognise later, for example | | Source type | | | Update frequency | | | Configuration | | The test data can change during development. Ask the team which version to use if you need reproducible results. The URL above pins an earlier commit. 5. Save the harvest source. 6. Trigger the harvest from the running container. Replace with the title, name, or harvest source ID you used. If CKAN generated a different URL name, use the last part of the harvest source URL. The test data contains non-resolvable URIs, so several errors are expected. If you get , rerun the harvest job and cancel the running job when prompted. This can happen because dataset and dataset series records are created in an order that is not deterministic. Set up nginx web server Start an nginx container to serve your DCAT files. Replace with the folder containing your test data. Verify the server is running by opening http://localhost:8888 in your browser. Connect to CKAN network Connect the nginx container to CKAN's Docker network so CKAN can access it: Now you can configure the harvest source in CKAN using the URL: Configure MIME types CKAN identifies DCAT files by their MIME types. Nginx does not include the correct MIME types for RDF formats by default. Add the following configuration to in the nginx container: Add this configuration inside the block, above the directive. Restart the container to apply the changes. CKAN can now harvest from your local web server.

developer-guide.title: 1 follow git workflow
developer-guide.content: Follow Git workflow Development on GDI follows a trunk-based development model with feature branches and specific commit message conventions to ensure consistency and clarity in contributions. Follow these guidelines when contributing code to any of the GDI repositories. Branching strategy Create feature branches from : Branch naming conventions: - - New features - - Bug fixes - - Documentation updates - - Code refactoring Commit messages Write clear, descriptive commit messages: Format: where type is: - - New feature - - Bug fix - - Documentation - - Tests - - Refactoring - - Maintenance Pull requests Push your branch and create a pull request on GitHub with description of changes and testing performed.

developer-guide.title: 2 request code review
developer-guide.content: Request code review Submit pull requests for code review before merging. Pull request checklist Before requesting review: - [ ] All tests pass - [ ] Code follows style guidelines - [ ] Documentation updated - [ ] SPDX headers added - [ ] No merge conflicts Write PR description Include: - What: Summary of changes - Why: Motivation and context - How: Implementation approach - Testing: How you verified changes Example: Respond to feedback - Address review comments promptly - Ask questions if feedback is unclear - Update PR with requested changes - Mark conversations as resolved when complete

developer-guide.title: 3 update documentation
developer-guide.content: Update documentation Keep documentation synchronised with code changes across inline comments, API docs, and user-facing guides. In this guide > When to update documentation > Write inline code comments > Document APIs with OpenAPI > Update user guides > Add SPDX licence headers When to update documentation Update documentation whenever you: - Add new features or APIs - Change existing behaviour - Fix bugs that affect usage - Update configuration options - Modify deployment procedures Write inline code comments Add comments to explain complex logic, business rules, or non-obvious implementations: Guidelines: - Focus on the "why", not the "what" - Keep comments concise and up to date - Use British English spelling Document APIs with OpenAPI GDI uses API-first development. API contracts are defined in OpenAPI YAML files located in before implementation. For details on working with OpenAPI specifications: - Add metadata fields: Shows the complete OpenAPI workflow including updating YAML files and regenerating code. - Add API endpoint: General guidance on API development. View generated API documentation at during development. Update user guides The documentation site uses Docusaurus, a modern static site generator built with React. Documentation structure Update the appropriate guide based on your changes: - : End-user documentation for data requesters and catalogue managers - : End-user documentation for catalogue managers (users of CKAN admin interface) - : Developer onboarding, setup, and contribution guides (you're here!) - : Deployment and operations documentation Run documentation locally Test your documentation changes locally before committing: The documentation site opens at with live reload. Write effective documentation Follow these guidelines: - Use sentence case: For headers (capitalise only the first word unless it's a proper noun) - Write in active voice: "Select the button" instead of "The button should be selected" - Use British English spelling: "organise" not "organize", "behaviour" not "behavior" - Keep it concise: Get to the point quickly - Add code examples: Show, don't just tell - Include screenshots: for UI changes (save in ) Markdown features Docusaurus supports MDX with additional components: Admonitions: Code blocks with syntax highlighting: typescript const greeting: string = "Hello, GDI!"; ` Tabs: Reference other pages Link to other documentation pages using relative paths or slugs: Add SPDX licence headers Add licence headers to all new files to comply with REUSE standards: For Markdown files: For code files: Check licence compliance with: Learn more - Documentation README - Technical details about the documentation site - Docusaurus documentation - Official Docusaurus guides - REUSE guidelines - Licence compliance standards

developer-guide.title: 4 publish a release
developer-guide.content: Publish a release Create and publish new releases across GDI repositories using the automated GitHub Actions workflow. This process applies to all GDI repositories. Understanding version numbering Releases follow semantic versioning : - Major - Breaking changes or major new features - Minor - New features, backwards compatible - Patch - Bug fixes and minor improvements Examples: , , Publish a release The release process is automated through GitHub Actions: 1. Go to the relevant repository on GitHub. See list of GDI repositories. 2. Select the Actions tab. 3. In the left panel under Workflows, select Publish Release. 4. Select Run workflow on the upper right corner of the workflow page. 5. In the Use workflow from dropdown, select Branch: main. 6. Enter the version increment. For example: . 7. Select Run workflow. The workflow will automatically: - Create the release tag - Create the release branch - Update the changelog - Build and publish Docker images - Create the GitHub release with generated notes Verify the release After the workflow completes, verify the release: - Check that the workflow completed successfully in the Actions tab - Verify the release appears on the releases page - Confirm Docker images were built and published correctly - Test the release in a staging environment if available If the workflow fails, check the GitHub Actions logs for errors and re-run the workflow after addressing any issues.
